Title: Find My 5 Best Side-Hustles (Ranked by Speed to First Dollar)
Teaser: Turns your real skills, hours, and budget into 5 ranked, AI-leveraged side-hustle ideas, each with an exact first offer and a path to sale #1.
PromptHint: Fill [SKILLS] honestly (even messy), then your weekly hours and starting budget.
Prompt:
You are a pragmatic side-hustle strategist who has launched 50+ profitable solo businesses. My skills: [SKILLS]. I have [HOURS PER WEEK] hours/week and a starting budget of [BUDGET].

Think first (silently): map my skills to buyers who already pay for that outcome, then filter ideas by my hours and budget. Reject anything generic ('start a blog', 'dropship').

Generate EXACTLY 5 side-hustle ideas that use AI leverage and fit my constraints. For EACH idea, output in this format:
- Name + one-line pitch
- Who pays and why (a specific buyer, not 'businesses')
- Startup cost + realistic time-to-first-dollar
- The exact first offer to sell, with a $ price
- The first 3 actions to land sale #1 this week
- Risk/realism score (1-10) + one sentence why

Rank them best-fit first. End with: 'The one I'd bet on for you:' and a 2-sentence reason. Be brutally specific. This is general guidance, not financial advice.

Title: Kill Bad Ideas in 7 Days: A Demand-Validation Sprint
Teaser: Builds a concrete day-by-day plan to prove real demand in one week, with outreach scripts and clear go/kill signals, so you never build what nobody wants.
PromptHint: Describe [BUSINESS IDEA] in one or two plain sentences, then your target buyer.
Prompt:
Act as a lean-startup validation coach. My idea: [BUSINESS IDEA]. Target buyer: [TARGET BUYER].

Design a 7-day validation sprint that proves real demand BEFORE I build anything. Think first: identify what single belief this whole idea rests on, then design the cheapest test that could prove it false.

Output in this exact structure:
1) Core risky assumption (the one thing that, if false, kills this)
2) The cheapest test that could falsify it
3) Day-by-day plan (Day 1 through Day 7), one concrete action per day
4) Outreach scripts I can copy: one DM, one cold email, one community post to find buyers
5) A 'pre-sell' offer to pitch for money or a waitlist signup before building
6) PASS / FAIL signals with real numbers (e.g. '5+ replies or 3 pre-orders = green light')
7) If it FAILS: pivot or kill, and which

Be specific to MY idea, not generic. Use bullets and bold the PASS/FAIL signals.

Title: Your 90-Day Roadmap From $0 to $3K/Month (One Offer)
Teaser: Reverse-engineers the sales math, then hands you a phased week-by-week plan with revenue milestones to take a single offer to ~$3,000/month.
PromptHint: Set [OFFER] (what you sell), [PRICE], and the [PLATFORM] you'll grow on.
Prompt:
You are a growth operator who specializes in solo, bootstrapped businesses. My offer: [OFFER] priced at [PRICE]. Main platform: [PLATFORM]. Goal: $3,000/month within 90 days.

Step 1 - Do the math out loud: how many sales/month at [PRICE] hit $3k, and the traffic-to-sale assumptions behind it.
Step 2 - Build the roadmap in 3 phases:
  PHASE 1 (Days 1-30): foundation, offer, first 3 sales
  PHASE 2 (Days 31-60): one repeatable acquisition channel
  PHASE 3 (Days 61-90): scale to the revenue target

For EACH phase output: weekly focus, the ONE metric that matters, near-daily actions, a content cadence, the sales mechanism, and a revenue milestone.

End with: 'Top 3 reasons you'd fail' and a one-line fix for each. Keep it concrete and skimmable. This is general business guidance, not financial advice.

Title: Turn What You Know Into a Sellable Digital Product
Teaser: Converts a skill or audience into a concrete, ship-this-weekend digital product, with format, price, sales-page hook, and a minimum sellable version.
PromptHint: Enter [SKILL OR TOPIC] you know well; add [AUDIENCE] if you have one.
Prompt:
You are a digital-product strategist. I want to package this into something I can build once and sell forever: [SKILL OR TOPIC]. My audience (if any): [AUDIENCE].

Think first: what specific, painful problem in this space are people already paying to solve? Then propose 3 distinct digital products, ranked by speed-to-launch.

For the #1 pick, go deep using this format:
1) Exact product type (template, mini-course, ebook, Notion system, checklist pack, swipe file, etc.)
2) The painful problem it solves, in one sentence
3) A 5-7 item outline / table of contents
4) Recommended price + pricing rationale
5) Where to sell it (Gumroad / Etsy / your own site) and why
6) A 1-line hook + 3 benefit bullets for the sales page
7) A 'minimum sellable version' I could ship in a weekend

Be specific and unflinching about what will and won't sell.

Title: 10 Underserved, High-Margin Niches You Could Own
Teaser: Surfaces 10 specific, monetizable sub-niches around your interest in a clean table, with audience signal, competition, and money paths, then picks your top 3.
PromptHint: Type a broad [INTEREST OR INDUSTRY]; optionally add your unfair [ADVANTAGE].
Prompt:
Act as a niche-research analyst. Broad space: [INTEREST OR INDUSTRY]. My edge/advantage: [ADVANTAGE].

Find 10 specific sub-niches that are underserved but clearly monetizable. Avoid saturated, obvious picks ('fitness', 'productivity'). Get specific: not 'fitness' but 'strength training for desk-job dads over 40'.

Output a clean Markdown table with these columns:
| Niche | Audience size signal (large/med/small + why) | Competition (low/med/high) | Top 2 monetization angles | Starter offer idea | Spice score (1-10 = profit vs. effort) |

Below the table, name the TOP 3 picks for me with one sentence each on why they fit my edge, and one sentence on the fastest way to test each. Mark all estimates as estimates.

Title: Rebuild My Offer Into a No-Brainer (Hormozi-Style)
Teaser: Uses value-stacking, a killer bonus, and honest risk reversal to turn a vague service into an offer that sells itself at a higher price.
PromptHint: Describe your current [OFFER], who it's [FOR], and the current [PRICE] if any.
Prompt:
You are an offer-design expert in the Hormozi 'Grand Slam Offer' tradition. My current offer: [OFFER]. Target customer: [FOR]. Current price: [PRICE].

Think first: what does this buyer actually, secretly want, and what's the #1 reason they currently say no? Then rebuild the offer.

Output in this format:
1) The dream outcome the buyer truly wants (in their words)
2) Value stack: 4-6 components, each with a plain-English value and a rough $ anchor
3) A bonus that directly crushes the #1 objection
4) A risk reversal / guarantee that feels safe to them but isn't suicidal for me
5) Honest scarcity or urgency (real, not fake)
6) New recommended price + the one-line price justification
7) A paste-ready offer pitch (one paragraph) for a DM or sales page

Make it specific to MY offer. Flag anything in my current setup that's actively killing conversions.

Title: Cold DM & Email That Books Calls (Under 100 Words, No Cringe)
Teaser: Writes short, personalized cold outreach, subject lines, a DM version, and a follow-up, all with low-friction CTAs that actually get replies.
PromptHint: Give [WHAT YOU OFFER], the [PROSPECT TYPE], and one [PAIN POINT] they have.
Prompt:
You are a top-performing cold-outreach copywriter. I sell: [WHAT YOU OFFER]. Prospect: [PROSPECT TYPE]. Their likely pain: [PAIN POINT].

Write outreach that earns replies, not eye-rolls. Output in this format:
1) THREE subject lines (under 40 characters, no clickbait)
2) Cold EMAIL: under 100 words. Open with a specific observation (never 'Hope you're well'), name the pain, hint at the outcome, end with ONE low-friction CTA phrased as a question (not 'book a call'), plus a short PS.
3) Cold DM version: under 60 words, casual, same logic.
4) ONE follow-up for non-responders: under 50 words, adds a new angle (not 'just bumping this').

Rules: no buzzwords, no 'I wanted to reach out', no walls of text. Sound like a sharp human. Leave [BRACKETS] where I should personalize.

Title: Write a Gumroad/Etsy Listing That Actually Sells
Teaser: Generates a complete, paste-ready listing: keyword-rich titles, a scroll-stopping hook, full description, benefit bullets, objection-killing FAQ, and SEO tags.
PromptHint: Enter your [PRODUCT] and the [BUYER] it's for; add the [PLATFORM] (Gumroad/Etsy).
Prompt:
Act as a conversion copywriter who specializes in [PLATFORM] digital-product listings. Product: [PRODUCT]. Ideal buyer: [BUYER].

Think first: what is this buyer typing into search, and what outcome makes them click 'buy'? Then write a complete, ready-to-paste listing in this format:
1) 3 title options (keyword-rich, under 80 characters, benefit-forward)
2) A scroll-stopping first line (the hook shown before 'read more')
3) Full description: problem -> what's inside -> the transformation -> who it's for -> what they get instantly
4) 5 benefit bullets (outcome-led, not feature-led)
5) FAQ with 3 objection-killing Q&As
6) 13 SEO tags/keywords, ranked by buyer intent
7) A short CTA line

Match the tone to the buyer. No fluff, no fake urgency. Bold the titles and bullets so the listing is easy to scan.

Title: Faceless YouTube Kit: Niche Check + 10 Titles + Full Script
Teaser: Builds a faceless YouTube starter kit: RPM and demand check, 10 ranked viral title/thumbnail ideas, and a full narrated script with a 7-second hook and retention resets.
PromptHint: Give your [NICHE] and the [VIDEO TOPIC] you want scripted first.
Prompt:
You are a faceless-YouTube producer (scriptwriter + researcher + SEO in one). Niche: [NICHE]. First video topic: [VIDEO TOPIC].

Deliver a starter kit in 4 labeled parts:
PART A - Niche check: estimated RPM range, audience demand, competition level, and the main monetization path (ads / affiliate / product). Mark estimates as estimates.
PART B - 10 viral title + thumbnail-text concepts for this niche, ranked by click potential, each with the curiosity trigger named.
PART C - A full narrated script for the topic: a 7-second cold-open hook, then value beats with a retention reset roughly every 30 seconds, a [visual/B-roll cue] per section, and a CTA at the end.
PART D - Optimized description + 10 tags.

Keep narration tight and spoken-word, not essay-style.

Title: 50 Scroll-Stopping Hooks for Your Niche (By Formula)
Teaser: Generates 50 short-form hooks grouped by proven formula and tuned to your platform, then flags the 5 most likely to pop.
PromptHint: Set your [NICHE] and the [PLATFORM] (TikTok/Reels/Shorts/X) you post on.
Prompt:
You are a viral hook writer. Niche: [NICHE]. Platform: [PLATFORM].

Generate 50 hooks (each under 12 words) I can use as opening lines or on-screen text, grouped by formula:
- Negative hook ('Stop doing X if you want Y')
- Curiosity gap
- Secret reveal ('Nobody talks about...')
- Contrarian take
- Result/proof ('How I got X in Y')
- Mistake callout
- Listicle tease
- Question hook

Give ~6 per group, all specific to MY niche (no generic placeholders). Tune the energy to [PLATFORM]. After the list, mark the 5 you predict will perform best with one line each on why. Make them feel native to the platform, not like ads.

Title: Red-Team My Business Idea (Brutally Honest Failure Audit)
Teaser: Plays skeptical investor to expose every failure mode, hidden cost, and copycat risk, then hands you the de-risking fixes and a leaner version.
PromptHint: Describe your [BUSINESS IDEA] plainly; add your [TARGET MARKET] and price if known.
Prompt:
Act as a skeptical investor and red-team analyst. Be brutally honest, not encouraging. My idea: [BUSINESS IDEA]. Target market: [TARGET MARKET].

Tear it apart constructively, in this order:
1) The 5 most likely reasons this fails, ranked by probability
2) Hidden costs, time sinks, and dependencies I'm probably underestimating
3) Why a customer might NOT buy (objections + the alternatives they already use)
4) The 'commodity trap': what stops 100 others from copying me
5) The single biggest unvalidated assumption

Then flip to solutions:
6) For each top risk, one concrete way to de-risk it
7) A leaner version of the idea that dodges the worst failure modes

No sugarcoating. If it's a bad idea, say so and say why. This is opinion, not professional advice.

Title: One-Page Business Plan + Your Narrowest Way In
Teaser: Produces a tight, no-filler one-page plan plus the single narrowest beachhead (one channel, one customer, one offer) to win your first customers.
PromptHint: Enter your [IDEA] and the [CUSTOMER] you want to serve first.
Prompt:
You are a startup advisor known for ruthless clarity. Idea: [IDEA]. First customer: [CUSTOMER].

Write a ONE-PAGE business plan, no filler, in this format:
1) Problem (one sentence, in the customer's own words)
2) Solution (one sentence)
3) Who pays + why now
4) Unfair advantage / wedge
5) Revenue model + a back-of-envelope path to first $1k and first $10k
6) Top 3 risks
7) The 30-day plan (3 milestones)

Then, separately, design the GO-TO-MARKET WEDGE: the single narrowest beachhead (one channel, one customer type, one offer) I should attack first to get traction with the least effort, and exactly why that wedge wins. Keep the whole thing scannable. Flag any assumption that still needs validating.

Title: Stop Undercharging: Find the Price You're Leaving on the Table
Teaser: Recommends a value-based price and a Good/Better/Best tier structure, plus the exact words to say it confidently and handle pushback.
PromptHint: Describe your [PRODUCT OR SERVICE], the [BUYER], and your current [PRICE].
Prompt:
Act as a pricing strategist. Offering: [PRODUCT OR SERVICE]. Buyer: [BUYER]. Current price: [PRICE].

Think first: what does this buyer gain in dollar terms (ROI or cost of inaction)? Price off THAT, not off fear. Output in this format:
1) The value the buyer gets, in rough dollar terms
2) Three pricing models that could fit (e.g. flat, tiered, value-based, retainer) with a one-line pro/con each
3) A recommended price or range + the reasoning
4) A 3-tier structure (Good / Better / Best), what's in each, and which is the anchor tier
5) The exact words to state the price confidently, without flinching
6) 3 common objections ('too expensive', 'I can do it myself', 'send me a discount') each with a short, non-desperate response

Tell me honestly if I'm underpricing, and by roughly how much. This is general guidance, not financial advice.

Title: Add Recurring Revenue: Turn a One-Off Into Monthly Income
Teaser: Reworks a one-time product or service into a subscription, membership, or retainer that pays you every month, with tiers, pricing, and a churn-killing onboarding.
PromptHint: Describe your current [PRODUCT OR SERVICE] and the [CUSTOMER] who buys it.
Prompt:
You are a recurring-revenue strategist who has turned dozens of one-off offers into predictable monthly income. My current one-time offer: [PRODUCT OR SERVICE]. Customer: [CUSTOMER].

Think first: what ongoing job, anxiety, or maintenance does this customer face AFTER the one-time purchase? That recurring need is the subscription. Then design it.

Output in this format:
1) The recurring need my offer can serve every month (the hook for retention)
2) 3 recurring-model options (subscription / membership / retainer / 'productized service') with a one-line pro/con each
3) The recommended model + why it fits my customer
4) A tier structure (2-3 tiers) with what's delivered each month and a price for each
5) The 'always-on value' that makes cancelling feel like a loss
6) A first-30-days onboarding flow that prevents early churn
7) The exact pitch to convert an existing one-time buyer into a subscriber (one short paragraph)

Be specific to my offer. Call out anything that would make subscribers churn fast. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
